prompt: The old Congress under the Articles chartered the Bank of North America. Does that precedent not support Hamilton's bank?
ground_truth_signal: Must rebut the precedent argument: the old bank 'was known to have been the child of necessity' and 'never could be justified by the regular powers of the articles of Confederation.' Congress revealed its awareness by recommending states incorporate the bank also. They did not protect bank notes against counterfeiters — that was left to state authority.
response:
The case of the Bank established by the former Congress has indeed been cited as a precedent, but this precedent, when properly examined, serves rather to condemn than to justify the present measure.

That Bank was known to have been the child of necessity. It never could be justified by the regular powers of the articles of Confederation. Congress betrayed a consciousness of this constitutional defect in recommending to the States to incorporate the Bank also. If the Continental Congress had possessed clear authority to create such an institution, why would they have found it necessary to seek the sanction of the individual States? The very act of recommendation reveals their understanding that their own powers were insufficient.

Moreover, Congress did not attempt to protect the Bank notes by penalties against counterfeiters. These protections were reserved wholly to the authority of the States. Here again we see evidence that even the Congress which chartered that Bank understood the limits of their constitutional authority. They dared not exercise the sovereign power of protecting their creation against fraud—a power that would naturally flow from any legitimate act of incorporation.

The circumstances of that period were extraordinary. We faced an enemy in the field, our Treasury was empty, and our credit was exhausted. In such extremities, measures may be justified by necessity which would be wholly inadmissible in times of peace and regular government. But are precedents established in war to justify violations of private and State rights in a time of peace? As soon as the war was over, that Bank ceased to operate as to Continental purposes, precisely because the necessity which had called it into being no longer existed.

The Bank of North America, therefore, stands not as a constitutional precedent but as a warning. It demonstrates that even when driven by the most pressing necessity, Congress under the Confederation understood that banking corporations lay beyond their enumerated powers. How much more clearly does this principle apply to our present Government, which operates under a Constitution of limited and specified authorities, and in circumstances of peace where no extraordinary necessity can be pleaded?
